programs.title: Diabolo
programs.desc: In China, the diabolo has a history of over 600 years. From the earliest uniaxial design, it developed to have a double axis. With time, it came to be made in various shapes and from various materials. And its popularity began to spread around the world. In the hands of an expert, a diabolo can create a truly mesmerizing spectacle. So, let’s hear from a professional about the various tricks of his trade.

programs.title: From Stone to Story
programs.desc: China's profound stone carving culture dates back to the Han Dynasty (202 BC – 220 AD) and flourished during the Tang Dynasty (618 – 907 AD), evolving into a distinctive art form and unique cultural practice. Among many places famous for stone carving, Hui’an County—through generations of dedicated artisans—has risen to prominence and earned the esteemed title of \"World Capital of Stone Carving,\" awarded by the World Crafts Council.\nThe county’s skilled stonemasons are celebrated for their ability to transform ordinary pieces of rock into beautiful works of art. This episode shines a spotlight on the intangible cultural heritage of Hui’an stone carving and reveals how it has been influenced by the area’s close proximity to the sea.

programs.title: Huayin Old Tunes: the Earliest Rock and Roll by the Yellow River
programs.desc: Huayin Laoqiang is the name given to a traditional form of shadow puppet show. It translates as “Huayin Old Tunes,” and originated in the chanting of the boatmen working at the confluence of the Yellow, Weihe, and Luohe Rivers, close to the city of Huayin in Shaanxi Province. This chanting evolved into folk songs which, with time, were illustrated with puppet shows. The loud music, rustic instruments, and enthusiastic performances epitomize the way the local people lead their lives.

programs.title: CULTURAL INHERITORS Colored stone inlay art: Transformation matters
programs.desc: The Vibe production team's meticulously crafted documentary series \"Cultural Inheritors\". The eight episodes span across various regions of China, focusing on the inheritors and artists of intangible cultural heritage. They explore and innovate with artisanal dedication, showcasing the extraordinary craftsmanship of fields such as the Ru Kiln porcelain, colored stone inlay, Pu'er tea, and Lingnan woodwork. Let’s embark on a cultural journey together, savoring the beauty of tradition and embracing the spirit of intangible cultural heritage.

programs.title: The Story of Flying Trident
programs.desc: Feicha, or a flying trident, is a type of performance where you balance a revolving trident on your body. In Chinese history, the trident was one of the 18 main weapons of war, but now it has become more of an acrobatic act. For thousands of years, people in China have practiced it as a performance and also as a way to keep fit.\nIt seems like the trident is stuck to the person’s body like a magnet, but that effect is achieved by tensing and relaxing muscles at the right time so that the movement of the trident is channeled in the right direction—be it around the hands, neck, or back. Otherwise, its energy will turn against the performer.

programs.title: Awakening Lion
programs.desc: Xingshi, or literally “awakened lion,” is a name for the southern style of the lion dance. Historically, it evolved from a court dance in the Tang Dynasty almost 1,200 years ago. With the southward migration of the population from the central plains, lion dance culture was introduced to the south. In the Ming Dynasty around 400 years ago, xingshi appeared in Guangdong and was then widely spread throughout southern China and even overseas. Lion dance has become a very important part of Chinese culture.
